**Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG**, commonly known as **Porsche**,[[a]](#cite_note-8) is a German automobile manufacturer specializing in luxury, high-performance [sports cars](/wiki/Sports_car "Sports car"), [SUVs](/wiki/SUVs "SUVs") and [sedans](/wiki/Sedans "Sedans"), headquartered in [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart"), Baden-Württemberg, Germany. The company is owned by [Volkswagen AG](/wiki/Volkswagen_Group "Volkswagen Group"), a controlling stake of which is owned by [Porsche Automobil Holding SE](/wiki/Porsche_SE "Porsche SE"), usually shortened to Porsche SE. Porsche's current lineup includes the [911](/wiki/Porsche_911 "Porsche 911"), [Panamera](/wiki/Porsche_Panamera "Porsche Panamera"), [Macan](/wiki/Porsche_Macan "Porsche Macan"), [Cayenne](/wiki/Porsche_Cayenne "Porsche Cayenne") and [Taycan](/wiki/Porsche_Taycan "Porsche Taycan")[[8]](#cite_note-9).

The origins of the company date to the 1930s when German Bohemian automotive engineer [Ferdinand Porsche](/wiki/Ferdinand_Porsche "Ferdinand Porsche") founded Porsche[[9]](#cite_note-Auto-10) with [Adolf Rosenberger](/wiki/Adolf_Rosenberger "Adolf Rosenberger"),[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[2]](#cite_note-DeJong-2) a key figure in the creation of German automotive manufacturer and [Audi](/wiki/Audi "Audi") precursor [Auto Union](/wiki/Auto_Union "Auto Union"),[[11]](#cite_note-12) and Austrian businessman [Anton Piëch](/wiki/Anton_Pi%C3%ABch "Anton Piëch"), who was, at the time, also Ferdinand Porsche's son in law. In its early days, it was contracted by the [German government](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") to create a vehicle for the masses, which later became the [Volkswagen Beetle](/wiki/Volkswagen_Beetle "Volkswagen Beetle").[[12]](#cite_note-:3-13) After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), when Ferdinand, a member of both the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") and the [SS](/wiki/SS "SS"), would be arrested for [war crimes](/wiki/War_crime "War crime"), his son [Ferry Porsche](/wiki/Ferry_Porsche "Ferry Porsche"), an SS volunteer, began building his own car, which would result in the [Porsche 356](/wiki/Porsche_356 "Porsche 356").

In 2009, Porsche entered an agreement with [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen") to create an 'integrated working group' by merging the two companies' car manufacturing operations.[[13]](#cite_note-PorscheMerger-14)[[14]](#cite_note-:4-15) By 2015, [Porsche SE](/wiki/Porsche_SE "Porsche SE"), the holding company spun off from the original Porsche firm, had a controlling interest in the Volkswagen Group, which included Audi and [Lamborghini](/wiki/Lamborghini "Lamborghini") as subsidiaries.[[15]](#cite_note-:5-16)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origin")]

[Ferdinand Porsche](/wiki/Ferdinand_Porsche "Ferdinand Porsche") (1875–1951) founded the company called "Dr. Ing. h. c. F. Porsche [GmbH](/wiki/GmbH "GmbH")"[[9]](#cite_note-Auto-10) with [Adolf Rosenberger](/wiki/Adolf_Rosenberger "Adolf Rosenberger")[[16]](#cite_note-17) and [Anton Piëch](/wiki/Anton_Pi%C3%ABch "Anton Piëch") in 1931.[[3]](#cite_note-Klawitter-3) The name is short for Ferdinand Porsche's full title in German, *[Doktor-Ingenieur](/wiki/Doktoringenieur "Doktoringenieur") [honoris causa](/wiki/Honorary_degree "Honorary degree")* (lit. 'Doctor of Engineering, Honorary Degree').[[17]](#cite_note-18) The main offices was at Kronenstraße 24 in the centre of [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart").[[18]](#cite_note-Historie-19) Initially, the company offered motor vehicle development work and consulting,[[9]](#cite_note-Auto-10) but did not build any cars under its own name. One of the first assignments the new company received was from the German government to design a car for the people; that is, a *[Volk](/wiki/Volk "Volk")swagen*.[[9]](#cite_note-Auto-10) This resulted in the [Volkswagen Beetle](/wiki/Volkswagen_Beetle "Volkswagen Beetle"), one of the most successful car designs of all time.[[12]](#cite_note-:3-13) Later, the [Porsche 64](/wiki/Porsche_64 "Porsche 64") would be developed in 1939 using many components from the Beetle.[[9]](#cite_note-Auto-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Elefant_USAOM-01.jpg/250px-Elefant_USAOM-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elefant_USAOM-01.jpg)

*[Panzerjäger Elefant](/wiki/Elefant "Elefant")* – a [tank destroyer](/wiki/Tank_destroyer "Tank destroyer") also known as "Ferdinand"[[19]](#cite_note-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Schwimmwagen_at_RAF_Duxford.jpg/250px-Schwimmwagen_at_RAF_Duxford.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schwimmwagen_at_RAF_Duxford.jpg)

The Porsche-designed Schwimmwagen is the most-produced amphibious car in history.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"),[[22]](#cite_note-Beetle-23) [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen") production turned to the military version of the Volkswagen Beetle, the [Kübelwagen](/wiki/Volkswagen_K%C3%BCbelwagen "Volkswagen Kübelwagen"),[[22]](#cite_note-Beetle-23) 52,000 produced, and [Schwimmwagen](/wiki/Schwimmwagen "Schwimmwagen"),[[22]](#cite_note-Beetle-23) 15,584 produced.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Porsche produced several designs for [heavy tanks](/wiki/Heavy_tank "Heavy tank") during the war, losing out to [Henschel & Son](/wiki/Henschel_%26_Son "Henschel & Son") in both contracts that ultimately led to the [Tiger I](/wiki/Tiger_I "Tiger I") and the [Tiger II](/wiki/Tiger_II "Tiger II"). However, not all this work was wasted, as the chassis Porsche designed for the Tiger I was used as the base for the [Elefant](/wiki/Elefant "Elefant") [tank destroyer](/wiki/Tank_destroyer "Tank destroyer"). Porsche also developed the [Maus](/wiki/Panzer_VIII_Maus "Panzer VIII Maus") [super-heavy tank](/wiki/Super-heavy_tank "Super-heavy tank") in the closing stages of the war, producing two prototypes.[[24]](#cite_note-25) Ferdinand Porsche's biographer, Fabian Müller, wrote that Porsche had thousands of people forcibly brought to work at their factories during the war. The workers wore the letter "P" on their clothing at all times. It stood not for "Porsche", but for "Poland".[[25]](#cite_note-26)

At the end of World War II in 1945, the [mercedesfactory](/wiki/List_of_Volkswagen_Group_factories "List of Volkswagen Group factories") at [KdF-Stadt](/wiki/KdF-Stadt "KdF-Stadt") fell to the British. Ferdinand lost his position as chairman of the board of management of Volkswagen, and [Ivan Hirst](/wiki/Ivan_Hirst "Ivan Hirst"), a British Army major, was put in charge of the factory.[[b]](#cite_note-28) On 15 December of that year, Ferdinand was arrested for war crimes, but not tried. During his 20-month imprisonment, Ferdinand Porsche's son, [Ferry Porsche](/wiki/Ferdinand_Anton_Ernst_Porsche "Ferdinand Anton Ernst Porsche"), decided to build his own car because he could not find an existing one that he wanted to buy. He also had to steer the company through some of its most difficult days until his father's release in August 1947.[[27]](#cite_note-Gmünd-29)

The first models of what was to become the [356](/wiki/Porsche_356 "Porsche 356") were built in a small sawmill in [Gmünd](/wiki/Gm%C3%BCnd,_Carinthia "Gmünd, Carinthia"), Austria.[[27]](#cite_note-Gmünd-29) The prototype car was shown to German auto dealers, and when pre-orders reached a set threshold, production (with aluminum body) was begun by [Porsche Konstruktionen GesmbH](/wiki/Porsche_Salzburg "Porsche Salzburg"), founded by Ferry and [Louise](/wiki/Louise_Pi%C3%ABch "Louise Piëch"). Many regard the 356 as the first Porsche simply because it was the first model *sold* by the fledgling company. After production of the 356 was taken over by the father's Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche GmbH in Stuttgart in 1950, Porsche commissioned a [Zuffenhausen](/wiki/Zuffenhausen "Zuffenhausen")-based company, Reutter Karosserie, which had previously collaborated with the firm on Volkswagen Beetle prototypes, to produce the 356's steel body. In 1952, Porsche constructed an assembly plant (Werk 2) across the street from Reutter Karosserie; the main road in front of Werk 1, the oldest Porsche building, is now known as Porschestraße.[[28]](#cite_note-30) The 356 was road-certified in 1948.

### Company logo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Company logo")]

* [![Coat of arms of Württemberg during the Weimar-era](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Wappen_Volksstaat_W%C3%BCrttemberg_%28Farbe%29.svg/120px-Wappen_Volksstaat_W%C3%BCrttemberg_%28Farbe%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wappen_Volksstaat_W%C3%BCrttemberg_(Farbe).svg "Coat of arms of Württemberg during the Weimar-era")

  [Coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") of [Württemberg](/wiki/Free_People%27s_State_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Free People's State of Württemberg") during the [Weimar-era](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic")
* [![Coat of arms of Stuttgart](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/DEU_Stuttgart_COA.svg/120px-DEU_Stuttgart_COA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:DEU_Stuttgart_COA.svg "Coat of arms of Stuttgart")

  [Coat of arms of Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart#Name_and_coat_of_arms "Stuttgart")

Porsche's company logo stems from the [coat of arms of the Free People's State of Württemberg](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Coat of arms of Württemberg") of [Weimar Germany](/wiki/Weimar_Germany "Weimar Germany") of 1918–1933, which had [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart") as its capital. (The [Bundesland](/wiki/Bundesland_(Germany) "Bundesland (Germany)") of [Württemberg-Hohenzollern](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Hohenzollern "Württemberg-Hohenzollern") used the same arms from 1945 to 1952, while Stuttgart during these years operated as the capital of adjacent [Württemberg-Baden](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Baden "Württemberg-Baden").) The arms of Stuttgart appear in the middle of the logo as an [inescutcheon](/wiki/Inescutcheon "Inescutcheon"), for the company had its headquarters in Stuttgart. The heraldic symbols, combined with the texts "Porsche" and "Stuttgart", do not form a conventional [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms"), since [heraldic achievements](/wiki/Achievement_(heraldry) "Achievement (heraldry)") never spell out the name of the [armiger](/wiki/Armiger "Armiger") nor the armiger's home town in the shield.

Württemberg-Baden and Württemberg-Hohenzollern, both in 1952, became part of the present Bundesland of [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg") after the political consolidation of [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") in 1949, but the old design of the arms of Württemberg lives on in the Porsche logo. On 30 January 1951, not long before the formation of Baden-Württemberg, Ferdinand Porsche died from complications following a stroke.

### Developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Developments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/1953-porsche-archives.jpg/250px-1953-porsche-archives.jpg)](/wiki/File:1953-porsche-archives.jpg)

1952 Porsche 356 K/9-1 prototype

In post-war Germany, parts were generally in short supply, so the 356 automobile used components from the Volkswagen Beetle, including the engine case from its [internal combustion engine](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine"), [transmission](/wiki/Transmission_(mechanics) "Transmission (mechanics)"), and several parts used in the [suspension](/wiki/Suspension_(vehicle) "Suspension (vehicle)"). The 356, however, had several evolutionary stages, A, B, and C, while in production, and most Volkswagen-sourced parts were replaced by Porsche-made parts. Beginning in 1954, the 356's engines started utilizing engine cases designed specifically for the 356. The sleek bodywork was designed by [Erwin Komenda](/wiki/Erwin_Komenda "Erwin Komenda"), who also had designed the body of the Beetle. Porsche's signature designs have, from the beginning, featured air-cooled rear-engine configurations (like the Beetle), rare for other car manufacturers, but producing automobiles that are very well balanced.

In 1964, after a fair amount of success in [motor-racing](/wiki/Auto_racing "Auto racing") with various models including the [550 Spyder](/wiki/Porsche_550 "Porsche 550"), and with the 356 needing a major re-design, the company launched the [Porsche 911](/wiki/Porsche_911 "Porsche 911"): another [air-cooled](/wiki/Air-cooled_engine "Air-cooled engine"), [rear-engined](/wiki/Rear-engine_design "Rear-engine design") sports car, this time with a six-cylinder ["boxer" engine](/wiki/Flat_engine "Flat engine"). The team to lay out the body shell design was led by Ferry Porsche's eldest son, [Ferdinand Alexander Porsche](/wiki/Ferdinand_Alexander_Porsche "Ferdinand Alexander Porsche") (F. A.). The design phase for the 911 caused internal problems with Erwin Komenda, who led the body design department until then. F. A. Porsche complained Komenda made unauthorized changes to the design. Company leader Ferry Porsche took his son's drawings to neighbouring chassis manufacturer Reuter. Reuter's workshop was later acquired by Porsche (so-called Werk 2). Afterward, Reuter became a seat manufacturer, today known as [Keiper-Recaro](/wiki/Recaro "Recaro").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Porsche_912_Coupe_in_the_Porsche-Museum_%282009%29_IMG_2730.jpg/250px-Porsche_912_Coupe_in_the_Porsche-Museum_%282009%29_IMG_2730.jpg)](/wiki/File:Porsche_912_Coupe_in_the_Porsche-Museum_(2009)_IMG_2730.jpg)

The [Porsche 912](/wiki/Porsche_912 "Porsche 912"), from the 1960s

The design office gave sequential numbers to every project (See [Porsche type numbers](/wiki/Porsche_type_numbers "Porsche type numbers")), but the designated 901 nomenclature contravened [Peugeot](/wiki/Peugeot "Peugeot")'s trademarks on all 'x0x' names, so it was adjusted to 911. Racing models adhered to the "correct" numbering sequence: 904, 906, 908. The 911 has become Porsche's most well-known model – successful on the race-track, in [rallies](/wiki/Rallying "Rallying"), and in terms of road car sales. It remains in production; however, after several generations of revision, current-model 911s share only the basic mechanical configuration of a rear-engined, six-cylinder [coupé](/wiki/Coup%C3%A9 "Coupé"), and basic styling cues with the original car. A cost-reduced model with the same body, but with a 356-derived four-cylinder engine, was sold as the 912.

In 1972, the company's legal form was changed from *[Kommanditgesellschaft](/wiki/Kommanditgesellschaft "Kommanditgesellschaft")* (KG), or limited partnership, to *[Aktiengesellschaft](/wiki/Aktiengesellschaft "Aktiengesellschaft")* (AG), or public limited company, because Ferry Porsche came to believe the scale of the company outgrew a "family operation", after learning about [Soichiro Honda](/wiki/Soichiro_Honda "Soichiro Honda")'s "no family members in the company" policy at [Honda](/wiki/Honda "Honda"). This led to the establishment of an executive board with members from outside the Porsche family, and a supervisory board consisting largely of family members. With this change, most family members in the operation of the company, including F. A. Porsche and Ferdinand Piëch departed from the company.

F. A. Porsche founded his own design company, [Porsche Design](/wiki/Porsche_Design "Porsche Design"), which is renowned for exclusive sunglasses, watches, furniture, and many other luxury articles. Louise's son and Ferry's nephew [Ferdinand Piëch](/wiki/Ferdinand_Pi%C3%ABch "Ferdinand Piëch"), who was responsible for mechanical development of Porsche's production and racing cars (including the very successful [911](/wiki/Porsche_911 "Porsche 911"), [908](/wiki/Porsche_908 "Porsche 908") and [917](/wiki/Porsche_917 "Porsche 917") models), formed his own engineering bureau, and developed a [five-cylinder-inline](/wiki/Straight-five_engine "Straight-five engine") [diesel engine](/wiki/Diesel_engine "Diesel engine") for [Mercedes-Benz](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz "Mercedes-Benz"). A short time later, he moved to [Audi](/wiki/Audi "Audi") (used to be a division, then a subsidiary, of Volkswagen), and pursued his career through the entire company, ultimately becoming the chairman of [Volkswagen Group](/wiki/Volkswagen_Group "Volkswagen Group").

The first chief executive officer (CEO) of Porsche AG was [Ernst Fuhrmann](/wiki/Ernst_Fuhrmann "Ernst Fuhrmann"), who had been working in the company's engine development division. Fuhrmann was responsible for the so-called Fuhrmann-engine, used in the 356 Carrera models as well as the 550 Spyder, having [four overhead camshafts](/wiki/Double_overhead_camshaft "Double overhead camshaft") instead of a central camshaft with pushrods, as in the Volkswagen-derived serial engines. He planned to cease the 911 during the 1970s and replace it with the [V8](/wiki/V8_engine "V8 engine")-[front engined](/wiki/Front-engine,_rear-wheel-drive_layout "Front-engine, rear-wheel-drive layout") grand sportswagon [928](/wiki/Porsche_928 "Porsche 928"). As we know today, the 911 outlived the 928 by far. Fuhrmann was replaced in the early 1980s by [Peter W. Schutz](/wiki/Peter_W._Schutz "Peter W. Schutz"), an American manager and self-proclaimed 911 aficionado. He was then replaced in 1988 by the former manager of German computer company [Nixdorf Computer AG](/wiki/Nixdorf_Computer "Nixdorf Computer"), [Arno Bohn](/w/index.php?title=Arno_Bohn&action=edit&redlink=1 "Arno Bohn (page does not exist)"), who made some costly miscalculations that led to his dismissal soon after, along with that of the development director, [Ulrich Bez](/wiki/Ulrich_Bez "Ulrich Bez"), who was formerly responsible for BMW's [Z1](/wiki/BMW_Z1 "BMW Z1") model, and was CEO of [Aston Martin](/wiki/Aston_Martin "Aston Martin") from 2000 to 2013.[[29]](#cite_note-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Classic_Moto_Show_2014_%28115%29.JPG/250px-Classic_Moto_Show_2014_%28115%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Classic_Moto_Show_2014_(115).JPG)

Porsche 911 ([964](/wiki/Porsche_964 "Porsche 964")), introduced in 1989, was the first to be offered with Porsche's [Tiptronic](/wiki/Tiptronic "Tiptronic") transmission and four-wheel drive.

In 1990, Porsche drew up a memorandum of understanding with [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota") to learn and benefit from Japanese [lean manufacturing](/wiki/Lean_manufacturing "Lean manufacturing") methods. In 2004, it was reported that Toyota was assisting Porsche with [hybrid technology](/wiki/Hybrid_electric_vehicle "Hybrid electric vehicle").[[30]](#cite_note-32)

Following the dismissal of Bohn, [Heinz Branitzki](/w/index.php?title=Heinz_Branitzki&action=edit&redlink=1 "Heinz Branitzki (page does not exist)"), a longtime Porsche employee, was appointed as interim CEO. Branitzki served in that position until [Wendelin Wiedeking](/wiki/Wendelin_Wiedeking "Wendelin Wiedeking") became CEO in 1993. Wiedeking took over the chairmanship of the board at a time when Porsche appeared vulnerable to a takeover by a larger company. During his long tenure, Wiedeking transformed Porsche into a very efficient and profitable company.

Ferdinand Porsche's nephew, Ferdinand Piëch, was chairman and CEO of the [Volkswagen Group](/wiki/Volkswagen_Group "Volkswagen Group") from 1993 to 2002 and is chairman of the Volkswagen AG Supervisory Board since then. With 12.8 percent of the Porsche SE voting shares, he also remains the second-largest individual shareholder of Porsche SE after his cousin, F. A. Porsche, which had 13.6 percent.

Porsche's 2002 introduction of the Cayenne also marked the unveiling of a new production facility in [Leipzig](/wiki/Leipzig "Leipzig"), [Saxony](/wiki/Free_State_of_Saxony "Free State of Saxony"), which once accounted for nearly half of Porsche's annual output. In 2004, production of the 456 [kilowatts](/wiki/Kilowatt "Kilowatt") (620 [PS](/wiki/Metric_horsepower "Metric horsepower"); 612 [bhp](/wiki/Horsepower#Brake_horsepower "Horsepower")) [Carrera GT](/wiki/Porsche_Carrera_GT "Porsche Carrera GT") commenced in Leipzig, and at EUR 450,000 ($440,000 in the United States), it was the most expensive production model Porsche ever built.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Toulousaine_de_l%27automobile_-_7425_-_Porsche_911_Carrera_%282011%29.jpg/250px-Toulousaine_de_l%27automobile_-_7425_-_Porsche_911_Carrera_%282011%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toulousaine_de_l%27automobile_-_7425_-_Porsche_911_Carrera_(2011).jpg)

Porsche 911 (991)

In mid-2006, after years of the Boxster (and later the Cayenne) as the best-selling Porsche in North America, the 911 regained its position as Porsche's best-seller in the region. The Cayenne and 911 have cycled as the top-selling model since. In Germany, the 911 outsells the Boxster/Cayman and Cayenne.[[31]](#cite_note-33)

In May 2011, Porsche Cars North America announced plans to spend $80–$100 million, but will receive about $15 million in economic incentives to move their North American headquarters from [Sandy Springs](/wiki/Sandy_Springs "Sandy Springs"), a suburb of [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), to [Aerotropolis Atlanta](/wiki/Aerotropolis_Atlanta "Aerotropolis Atlanta"), a new mixed-use development on the site of the old [Ford Hapeville plant](/wiki/Atlanta_Assembly "Atlanta Assembly") adjacent to [Hartsfield–Jackson Atlanta International Airport](/wiki/Hartsfield%E2%80%93Jackson_Atlanta_International_Airport "Hartsfield–Jackson Atlanta International Airport").[[32]](#cite_note-34) Designed by architectural firm [HOK](/wiki/Hellmuth,_Obata_and_Kassabaum "Hellmuth, Obata and Kassabaum"), the headquarters will include a new office building and test track.[[33]](#cite_note-35)[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-37) The facility will be known by its new address, One Porsche Drive.

In October 2017, Porsche Cars North America announced the launch of Porsche Passport,[[36]](#cite_note-38) a new sports [car and SUV subscription program](/wiki/Vehicle_subscription "Vehicle subscription"). This new offering allows consumers to access Porsche vehicles through subscribing to the service, rather than owning or leasing a vehicle. The Porsche Passport service was available initially in [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"),[[37]](#cite_note-39)[[38]](#cite_note-40) and has become available in many major cities across the US.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

During the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), in March 2020, Porsche suspended its manufacturing in Europe for two weeks: "By taking this step, the sports car manufacturer is responding to the significant acceleration in the rate of infection caused by the coronavirus and the resultant measures implemented by the relevant authorities."[[40]](#cite_note-42)

In August 2022, *[Bloomberg News](/wiki/Bloomberg_News "Bloomberg News")* reported that Porsche has lined up interest in subscription of its [initial public offering](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering") for a valuation between US$60–85 billion. It is expected to be listed on [Frankfurt Stock Exchange](/wiki/Frankfurt_Stock_Exchange "Frankfurt Stock Exchange") in September.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

### Relationship with Volkswagen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Relationship with Volkswagen")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Vw-porsche914-typenbezeichnung.JPG/250px-Vw-porsche914-typenbezeichnung.JPG)](/wiki/File:Vw-porsche914-typenbezeichnung.JPG)

Combined badging of the European 914

The company has always had a close relationship with, initially, the [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen") (VW) [marque](/wiki/Marque "Marque"), and later, the [Volkswagen Group](/wiki/Volkswagen_Group "Volkswagen Group") (which also owns [Audi AG](/wiki/Audi "Audi")), because the first [Volkswagen Beetle](/wiki/Volkswagen_Beetle "Volkswagen Beetle") was designed by [Ferdinand Porsche](/wiki/Ferdinand_Porsche "Ferdinand Porsche").

The two companies collaborated in 1969 to make the [VW-Porsche 914 and 914-6](/wiki/Porsche_914 "Porsche 914"), whereby the 914-6 had a Porsche engine, and the 914 had a Volkswagen engine. Further collaboration in 1976 resulted in the [Porsche 912](/wiki/Porsche_912 "Porsche 912")E (US only) and the [Porsche 924](/wiki/Porsche_924 "Porsche 924"), which used many Audi components, and was built at Audi's [Neckarsulm](/wiki/Neckarsulm "Neckarsulm") factory, which had been [NSU](/wiki/NSU_Motorenwerke "NSU Motorenwerke")'s. [Porsche 944s](/wiki/Porsche_944 "Porsche 944") were also built there,[[42]](#cite_note-44) although they used far fewer Volkswagen components. The Cayenne, introduced in 2002, shares its chassis with the [Volkswagen Touareg](/wiki/Volkswagen_Touareg "Volkswagen Touareg") and the [Audi Q7](/wiki/Audi_Q7 "Audi Q7"), which is built at the [Volkswagen Group factory](/wiki/List_of_Volkswagen_Group_factories "List of Volkswagen Group factories") in [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava"), [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia").

Porsche SE was created in June 2007 by renaming the old Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG, and became a holding company for the families' stake in Porsche Zwischenholding GmbH (50.1%) (which in turn held 100% of the old Porsche AG) and Volkswagen AG (50.7%).[[43]](#cite_note-45)[[14]](#cite_note-:4-15) At the same time, the new Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG (**Porsche AG**) was created for the car manufacturing business.

In August 2009, Porsche SE and [Volkswagen AG](/wiki/Volkswagen_Group "Volkswagen Group") reached an agreement that the car manufacturing operations of the two companies would merge in 2011, to form an "Integrated Automotive Group".[[13]](#cite_note-PorscheMerger-14)[[44]](#cite_note-VWAG_merger-46) The management of Volkswagen AG agreed to 50.76% of Volkswagen AG being owned by Porsche SE in return for Volkswagen AG management taking Porsche SE management positions (in order for Volkswagen management to remain in control), and for Volkswagen AG acquiring ownership of Porsche AG.

As of the end of 2015, the 52.2% control interest in Volkswagen AG is the predominant investment by Porsche SE, and VW AG in turn controls brands and companies such as [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen"), [Audi](/wiki/Audi "Audi"), [SEAT](/wiki/SEAT "SEAT"), [Škoda](/wiki/%C5%A0koda_Auto "Škoda Auto"), [Bentley](/wiki/Bentley "Bentley"), [Bugatti](/wiki/Bugatti "Bugatti"), [Lamborghini](/wiki/Lamborghini "Lamborghini"), Porsche AG, [Ducati](/wiki/Ducati "Ducati"), [Volkswagen Commercial Vehicles](/wiki/Volkswagen_Commercial_Vehicles "Volkswagen Commercial Vehicles"), [Scania](/wiki/Scania_AB "Scania AB"), [MAN](/wiki/MAN_SE "MAN SE"), as well as Volkswagen Financial Services.[[15]](#cite_note-:5-16)

Porsche AG, as a 100% subsidiary of VW AG, is responsible for the actual production and manufacture of the Porsche automobile line. The company currently produces [Porsche 911](/wiki/Porsche_911 "Porsche 911"),[[45]](#cite_note-47) [Boxster](/wiki/Porsche_Boxster "Porsche Boxster") and [Cayman](/wiki/Porsche_Cayman "Porsche Cayman") sports cars, the [Cayenne](/wiki/Porsche_Cayenne "Porsche Cayenne") and [Macan](/wiki/Porsche_Macan "Porsche Macan") [sport utility vehicles](/wiki/Sport_utility_vehicles "Sport utility vehicles") and the four-door [Panamera](/wiki/Porsche_Panamera "Porsche Panamera").

In addition to its subsidiaries [Porsche Design](/wiki/Porsche_Design "Porsche Design") and [Porsche Engineering](/wiki/Porsche_Engineering "Porsche Engineering"), Porsche AG owns the consulting firm [MHP Management- und IT-Beratung](/wiki/MHP_Management-_und_IT-Beratung "MHP Management- und IT-Beratung"), which was fully acquired by January 2024.[[46]](#cite_note-48) Porsche also has a 29% share in German engineering and design consultancy [Bertrandt AG](/w/index.php?title=Bertrandt&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bertrandt (page does not exist)").[[47]](#cite_note-49)[[48]](#cite_note-50) In 2018, Porsche acquired a 10% minority shareholding stake of the Croatian [electric sportscar](/wiki/Electric_car "Electric car") manufacturer [Rimac Automobili](/wiki/Rimac_Automobili "Rimac Automobili") to form a development partnership.[[49]](#cite_note-Porsche_20_June_2018-51)[[50]](#cite_note-52)

### Initial public offering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Initial public offering")]

In February 2022, Volkswagen AG announced that it would examine the feasibility of a possible [IPO](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering") of Porsche AG.[[51]](#cite_note-:0-53) The [share capital](/wiki/Share_capital "Share capital") of Porsche AG has been divided into 50% [non-voting](/wiki/Non-voting_stock "Non-voting stock") [preference shares](/wiki/Preferred_stock "Preferred stock") and 50% ordinary shares.[[51]](#cite_note-:0-53)[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7) Volkswagen AG will retain 75% of ordinary shares, while [Porsche SE](/wiki/Porsche_SE "Porsche SE") will acquire 25% of ordinary shares. Volkswagen AG will also retain 75% of preference shares, while 25% of preference shares (12.5% of share capital) will be sold during IPO,[[51]](#cite_note-:0-53) while [Qatar Investment Authority](/wiki/Qatar_Investment_Authority "Qatar Investment Authority") has already committed to buy 4.99% of preference shares, leaving another 20.01% (10% of share capital), to other investors.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7) As part of the preliminary offering, 113,875 thousand shares were sold at the upper limit of the price range - 82.5 euros. Thus, the value of the company was estimated at 75 billion euros. In the first hours of trading on the [Frankfurt Stock Exchange](/wiki/Frankfurt_Stock_Exchange "Frankfurt Stock Exchange") on 29 September, the share price rose to 84 euros.[[52]](#cite_note-54)

### Australian eFuel operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Australian eFuel operations")]

Main article: [Highly Innovative Fuels Australia](/wiki/Highly_Innovative_Fuels_Australia "Highly Innovative Fuels Australia")

In April 2022, Porsche Australia announced they are planning to open an e-fuel manufacturing facility in the island state of [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania"). The plant will be the first of its type in the country. The facility is to be named the **HIF (Highly Innovative Fuels) Tasmania Carbon Neutral eFuel Plant**. It is slated to open in 2026.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

## Production and sales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Production and sales")]

The headquarters and main factory are located in [Zuffenhausen](/wiki/Zuffenhausen "Zuffenhausen"), a district in [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart"), where Porsche produces flat-6 and V8 piston engines.[[60]](#cite_note-62) Cayenne and Panamera models are manufactured in [Leipzig](/wiki/Leipzig "Leipzig"), Germany, and parts for the SUV are also assembled in the [Volkswagen Touareg](/wiki/Volkswagen_Touareg "Volkswagen Touareg") factory in [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava"), Slovakia.[[61]](#cite_note-Reuters-63) Boxster and Cayman production was outsourced to [Valmet Automotive](/wiki/Valmet_Automotive "Valmet Automotive") in Finland from 1997 to 2011, and in 2012 production moved to Germany.[[62]](#cite_note-valmet-automotive.com-64) Since 2011, the area of the Zuffenhausen plant has more than doubled, from 284,000 m2 (28.4 ha) to 614,000 m2 (61.4 ha), as a result of purchasing the former Layher, Deltona and Daimler sites, among others.[[63]](#cite_note-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66)

In 2015, Porsche reported selling a total of 218,983 cars, 28,953 (13.22%) as domestic German sales, and 190,030 (86.78%) internationally.[[65]](#cite_note-67)

The company has been highly successful in recent times, and indeed claims to have the highest profit per unit sold of any car company in the world.[[66]](#cite_note-68) Table of profits (in millions of euros) and number of cars produced. Figures from 2008/9 onwards were not reported as part of Porsche SE.[[67]](#cite_note-porsche-se1-69)

On 11 May 2017, Porsche built the one-millionth 911. An Irish green Carrera S was built for the celebration, and it will be taken on a global tour before becoming a permanent exhibit at the [Porsche Museum](/wiki/Porsche_Museum "Porsche Museum") in Stuttgart.[[68]](#cite_note-70)

In August 2021, Porsche confirmed that it would be setting up a production plant in Malaysia, the first country outside of Europe.[[69]](#cite_note-71) Local assembly will be handled by Porsche Malaysia's partner, Sime Darby, which has been the official distributor of the Stuttgart-based company in Malaysia since 2010.

In May 2025, Porsche North America confirmed the rumors that "production for all current 718 Boxster and 718 Cayman variants, including RS models, is scheduled to end in October of 2025".[[70]](#cite_note-72) Porsche CEO Oliver Blume has confirmed future production of full-electric replacements but said they will arrive in the "medium term."[[71]](#cite_note-73)

### Production composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Production composition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Porsche_Production_Statistics_2018.jpg/250px-Porsche_Production_Statistics_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Porsche_Production_Statistics_2018.jpg)

Porsche production statistics (2018)

Of the 255,683 cars produced in 2017, 33,820 were 911 models, 26,427 were Boxster and Cayman cars, 59,068 were Cayennes, 37,605 were Panameras and 98,763 were Macans.[[80]](#cite_note-82)

Of the 268,691 cars produced in 2018, 36,236 were 911 models, 23,658 were 718 Boxster and Cayman cars, 79,111 were Cayennes, 35,493 were Panameras, 93,953 were Macans, and 240 Taycan pre-series vehicles.[[81]](#cite_note-PorscheAG-Annual_Report2018-83)

Of the 263,236 cars produced in 2020, 28,672 were 911 models, 22,655 were 718 Boxster and Cayman cars, 82,137 were Cayennes, 21,832 were Panameras, 78,490 were Macans, and 29,450 were Taycan vehicles.[[82]](#cite_note-84)

Of the 321,321 vehicles produced in 2022, 41,947 were 911 models, 18,080 were 718 Boxster and Cayman models, 91,117 were Macans, 98,113 were Cayennes, 35,241 were Panameras, and 36,823 were Taycan models.[[79]](#cite_note-FY2022-81)

Of the 302,750 vehicles produced in 2024, 49,095 were 911 models, 23,790 were 718 Boxster and Cayman models, 84,330 were Macans, 93,864 were Cayennes, 30,369 were Panameras, and 21,302 were Taycan models.[[6]](#cite_note-FY2024-6)

### Hybrid and electric vehicles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Hybrid and electric vehicles")]

For details on a Porsche 911-based all-electric car, see [ERuf Model A](/wiki/ERuf_Model_A "ERuf Model A").

In 2010, Porsche launched the [Cayenne S Hybrid](/wiki/Porsche_Cayenne_S_Hybrid "Porsche Cayenne S Hybrid") and announced the [Panamera S Hybrid](/wiki/Porsche_Panamera_S_Hybrid "Porsche Panamera S Hybrid"), and launched the [Porsche 918](/wiki/Porsche_918 "Porsche 918") sports car in 2014, which also features a hybrid system. Also a plug-in hybrid model called the [Panamera S E-Hybrid](/wiki/Porsche_Panamera_S_E-Hybrid "Porsche Panamera S E-Hybrid") was released in October 2013 in the United States[[105]](#cite_note-107)[[106]](#cite_note-108) and during the fourth quarter of 2013 in several European countries.

Porsche developed a prototype electric [Porsche Boxster](/wiki/Porsche_Boxster "Porsche Boxster") called the Boxster E in 2011[[107]](#cite_note-109) and a hybrid version of the 911 called the GT3 R Hybrid, developed with [Williams Grand Prix Engineering](/wiki/Williams_Grand_Prix_Engineering "Williams Grand Prix Engineering") in 2010.[[108]](#cite_note-110)

In July 2014, Porsche announced the launch by the end of 2014 of the [Porsche Cayenne S E-Hybrid](/wiki/Porsche_Cayenne_S_E-Hybrid "Porsche Cayenne S E-Hybrid") a [plug-in hybrid](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid"), which will displace the Cayenne S Hybrid from the lineup. The S E-Hybrid will be the first plug-in hybrid in the premium [SUV](/wiki/SUV "SUV") segment and will allow Porsche to become the first automaker with three production plug-in hybrid models.[[109]](#cite_note-CayennePHEV-111)

In July 2017, Porsche installed its first 350 kW, 800V charging station, which the upcoming [Porsche Mission E](/wiki/Porsche_Taycan "Porsche Taycan") will use. As of 2017, the Porsche charging station is the fastest electric vehicle charging station in the world, being able to charge a Porsche Mission E up to 80% within 15 minutes. Porsche is also currently working with other manufacturers to make Porsche charging stations compatible with other electric vehicles.[[110]](#cite_note-112)

In August 2018, Porsche announced that the formerly named [Mission E](/wiki/Porsche_Taycan "Porsche Taycan") electric car would be named "*Taycan*", meaning 'leaping horse'.[[111]](#cite_note-113) The prototype electric car was expected to be revealed in 2019 after its completion.[[112]](#cite_note-114) Porsche Taycan went on sale in 2020.

### Electric bicycles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Electric bicycles")]

In 2021, Porsche released an electric mountain bike in partnership with [Rotwilde](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rotwild_(Fahrradhersteller) "de:Rotwild (Fahrradhersteller)"), with features such as an integrated cockpit designed by [Magura GmbH](/wiki/Magura_GmbH "Magura GmbH") and drive unit, drivetrain, and electronic shifting designed by [Shimano](/wiki/Shimano "Shimano").[[113]](#cite_note-115) Porsche followed this by completely buying eBike motor and battery company Fazua in 2022[[114]](#cite_note-116) and sports bikes company Greyp Bikes, sister company of [Rimac Automobili](/wiki/Rimac_Automobili "Rimac Automobili"), in 2023. Greyp Bikes was renamed to Porsche eBike Performance GmbH.[[115]](#cite_note-117)

Porsche has a record 19 outright wins at the [24 Hours of Le Mans](/wiki/24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "24 Hours of Le Mans").[[116]](#cite_note-118) Porsche is currently the world's largest race car manufacturer. ## Pronunciation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Pronunciation")]

In keeping with the family name of founder [Ferdinand Porsche](/wiki/Ferdinand_Porsche "Ferdinand Porsche"), the company's name is pronounced [[ˈpɔʁʃə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") in German, which corresponds to [/ˈpɔːrʃə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-uk-Porsche.ogg "File:En-uk-Porsche.ogg") [*POR-shə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") in English,[[118]](#cite_note-120) [homophonous](/wiki/Homophone "Homophone") with the feminine name *[Portia](/wiki/Portia_(disambiguation) "Portia (disambiguation)")*. However, in English it is often pronounced as a single syllable [/pɔːrʃ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Porsche.ogg "File:En-us-Porsche.ogg") [*PORSH*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")—without a final /[ə](/wiki/Mid_central_vowel "Mid central vowel")/. In [German orthography](/wiki/German_orthography "German orthography"), word-final ⟨e⟩ is not [silent](/wiki/Silent_e "Silent e") but is instead an [unstressed](/wiki/Unstressed_vowel "Unstressed vowel") [schwa](/wiki/Schwa "Schwa").

## Reputation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Reputation")]

In a survey conducted by the [Luxury Institute](/wiki/The_Luxury_Institute "The Luxury Institute") in New York, Porsche was awarded the title of "the most prestigious automobile brand". Five hundred households with a gross annual income of at least $200,000 and a net worth of at least $720,000 participated.[[119]](#cite_note-121)

Porsche won the [J.D. Power and Associates](/wiki/J.D._Power_and_Associates "J.D. Power and Associates") Initial Quality Study (IQS) in 2006, 2009, 2010, and 2014.[[120]](#cite_note-122)

### SUV reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: SUV reception")]

Porsche's 2003 SUV, the Cayenne, received generally favorable commentary.[[121]](#cite_note-CNBC-123)[[122]](#cite_note-Frankel-124)

In 2015, US News ranked the Macan as the best luxury compact SUV in its class.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

## Reliability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Porsche&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Reliability")]

A Canadian study in 2011 revealed that 97.4 percent of Porsches from the last 25 years are still on the road.[[124]](#cite_note-126)

In 2014, the [Cayman](/wiki/Porsche_Cayman "Porsche Cayman") and [Boxster](/wiki/Boxster "Boxster") made the *[Consumer Reports](/wiki/Consumer_Reports "Consumer Reports")* list for most reliable vehicles on the road.[[125]](#cite_note-127)

Porsche's 911 has been officially named by the [TÜV](/wiki/T%C3%9CV "TÜV") (Technischer Überwachungsverein; Technical Inspection Association) as Germany's most reliable car.[[126]](#cite_note-128)
